lesson: L29 — Right-Triangle Trigonometry: Sine, Cosine & Tangent
banner: Week 33 · Prove It — Why the Trig Ratios Never Change
essential_question: Why do sin θ, cos θ, and tan θ depend only on the angle θ — not on how big the right triangle is?
objective: Explain, using similar triangles, why the trigonometric ratios depend only on the angle and not on the triangle's size; set up and solve for a missing side using sin, cos, or tan; review the week's Math-U-See practice; and pass the mastery checkpoint on right-triangle trigonometry.

content.prompt: Without looking, draw a right triangle, label one acute angle θ, and label its opposite side, adjacent side, and hypotenuse. Write SOH-CAH-TOA from memory. Then explain out loud, in your own words, why the opposite and adjacent sides swap if you pick the OTHER acute angle instead.

content.heading: Go over Lesson Practice 29A & 29B together
content.note: Bring your completed MUS Lesson Practice 29A/29B. We check the tricky ones: reducing a ratio fully (like 6/10 down to 3/5), matching each side to sin, cos, or tan correctly for BOTH acute angles θ and α in the same triangle, and noticing that sin θ = cos α whenever θ and α are the two acute angles of the same right triangle.

content.heading: Defend three claims
bullets: Claim 1: sin θ, cos θ, and tan θ depend only on θ, never on the size of the triangle. Argue it from similar triangles — any two right triangles sharing acute angle θ are similar by AA (they share the right angle AND θ, so the third angle matches too), and similar triangles have proportional sides — so opposite/hypotenuse comes out to the same number no matter which similar triangle you measure.

Claim 2: if θ and α are the two acute angles of the same right triangle, then sin θ = cos α (and cos θ = sin α). Argue why, using the fact that the side opposite θ is the same side that's adjacent to α, and both ratios divide by the same shared hypotenuse.

Claim 3: given one side and an angle, you can't just guess which ratio to use — argue how to pick sin, cos, or tan correctly by naming what you're given relative to θ (opposite? adjacent? hypotenuse?) and what's being asked for.

content.preview: Next week, Lesson 30 — The Six Trigonometric Ratios — where sine, cosine, and tangent get three reciprocal partners: cosecant, secant, and cotangent.

success_criteria.text: I can explain, using similar triangles (AA), why sin θ, cos θ, and tan θ depend only on the angle θ, not the triangle's size
I can set up and solve for a missing side of a right triangle using sin, cos, or tan, given one side and an acute angle
I can find sin θ, cos θ, and tan θ as reduced fractions from a right triangle's three side lengths, and connect sin θ = cos α for the triangle's two acute angles

compass.note: Curriculum/Geometry/The Salas Compass/2026-W33 Sine, Cosine & Tangent.md

questions.prompt: Two right triangles both have a 35° acute angle but are different sizes. What must be true about sin 35° in each triangle?
options: It's different in each triangle, since the triangles are different sizes
It's exactly the same number in both triangles
It can only be found using a calculator, not from the sides
It depends on which triangle has the longer hypotenuse
questions.answer: It's exactly the same number in both triangles
questions.explain: Any two right triangles sharing an acute angle of 35° are similar by AA (both have a 90° angle and a 35° angle, so their third angles match too) — similar triangles have proportional sides, so opposite/hypotenuse is the same ratio in both, no matter the triangle's size.

questions.prompt: Because two right triangles with the same acute angle θ are similar by AA, their corresponding sides are…
questions.explain: Similar triangles don't have equal sides, but their corresponding sides always share the same ratio — that's exactly why sin θ, cos θ, and tan θ are fixed numbers for a given θ.

questions.prompt: A right triangle has legs 9 and 12 and a hypotenuse of 15. For the acute angle θ opposite the side of length 9, find sin θ, cos θ, and tan θ, fully reduced.
questions.answer: sin θ = 3/5, cos θ = 4/5, tan θ = 3/4
questions.explain: sin θ = 9/15 = 3/5; cos θ = 12/15 = 4/5; tan θ = 9/12 = 3/4 — the same 3-4-5 family as before, just scaled up by 3.

questions.prompt: In the triangle from #c3 (legs 9 and 12, hypotenuse 15), let α be the OTHER acute angle (opposite the side of length 12). Find sin α and cos α, and state which of θ's ratios each one matches.
questions.answer: sin α = 12/15 = 4/5 (matches cos θ); cos α = 9/15 = 3/5 (matches sin θ)
questions.explain: θ and α are complementary (they add to 90°); the side opposite one angle is the side adjacent to the other, and both share the same hypotenuse, so sin α = cos θ and cos α = sin θ.

questions.prompt: Set up and solve: a right triangle has a hypotenuse of 18 and an acute angle of 42°. Find the length of the side adjacent to the 42° angle, rounded to the nearest hundredth.
questions.explain: cos 42° = adjacent/18, so adjacent = 18 cos 42° ≈ 18(0.7431) ≈ 13.38 — CAH pairs adjacent with hypotenuse.

questions.prompt: The figure shows a right triangle with an acute angle marked θ ≈ 37°, an adjacent leg of 8 units, and an unknown opposite leg x. Use a ruler to measure x directly on the grid, then check: does it roughly match x = 8 tan 37° ≈ 6.03?
questions.answer: ≈6.0 units, matching the computed x = 8 tan 37° ≈ 6.03
questions.explain: Rubric: accept any ruler measurement within about half a unit of 6.03 — this confirms tan θ = opposite/adjacent is a real, checkable ratio, not just algebra.

questions.prompt: Close: rate yourself on Lesson 29 (Emerging / Proficient / Mastered) and name the one idea you'd want to revisit.
